The universe does not exist by accident or by some impersonal force. It exists because God spoke. "Let there be light"—and there was light. Three Hebrew words convey a staggering reality: God's word does not suggest, propose, or attempt. It accomplishes. What God declares is. The opening of Scripture places you not in a cosmos that evolved into being through mindless processes, but under the sovereign rule of a God whose creative power operates through His spoken word. Before atoms, before energy, before time itself, there was God. And because of Him, there is everything else. This is not poetry meant to inspire wonder (though it does). This is a declaration about reality itself. You live in a created universe, which means you live in a universe with order, purpose, and an Author.

What is striking about God's work across these six days is not merely the magnitude of creation but God's repeated evaluation: it is good. After light appears, after sky divides waters, after earth sprouts vegetation, after creatures fill the seas and sky—each time, God sees what He has made and affirms its goodness. This word "good" is not a casual approval. It means fitting, proper, aligned with God's design. And the repetition hammers home a truth that cuts against despair: your physical existence matters. Your body, the earth beneath your feet, the water, the animals, the food you eat—none of these are mistakes or regrettable compromises. They are expressions of God's intentional design and His pleasure. When God finally creates humanity in His own image—a distinction so profound that He uses the plural "Us" to describe it—He does not diminish the material world. He crowns it. You are called to rule over creation and subdue it, not to escape it or despise it.

But you must reckon with what it means to be made in God's image. This is not about your intelligence or your moral capacity alone. To be made in God's image means you bear His likeness in a way that no other creature does. You have the capacity to know God, to respond to Him, to create, to love, to choose. God gives you dominion—authority—over creation. Yet this is not domination driven by appetite or cruelty. God Himself has provided abundantly. He gives you the plants and their seeds for food and gives the beasts of the earth the green plants for food. There is provision built into creation from the start. What God grants is generous and good. Your authority is meant to reflect His character, not your own unbridled will. This is the foundation upon which all that follows in Scripture rests. You are not an evolved accident in an uncaring universe. You are a creature made by God, for God, bearing His image and accountable to Him.
